page.content:
The analysis of neonatal care outcomes is critical for improving healthcare strategies in maternity wards. The data presented in the accompanying table highlights the mean birth weights of infants admitted to two distinct care settings: Kangaroo Ward Care (KWC) and Intermediate Intensive Care (IIC). Notably, the mean birth weight for infants in KWC is reported at 953.66 grams, with a standard deviation of 127.9 grams, while infants in the IIC exhibit a higher mean birth weight of 984.86 grams, accompanied by a standard deviation of 113.3 grams. 

These findings suggest a significant difference in birth weights between the two care environments, which may reflect varying levels of medical intervention and support provided to neonates.

with a standard deviation of 127.9 grams, while infants in the IIC exhibit a higher mean birth weight of 984.86 grams,

accompanied by a standard deviation of 113.3 grams. 

These findings suggest a significant difference in birth weights between
the two care environments, which may reflect varying levels of medical intervention and support provided to neonates.

cells.cell_text: Mean Time to reach full feeds (days)

page.content: The KWC approach, which emphasizes skin-to-skin contact and family involvement, may cater more effectively to specific populations, potentially influencing developmental outcomes. In contrast, the IIC setting appears to accommodate infants requiring more intensive medical supervision, underscoring the importance of tailored care strategies. This data serves as a foundation for further investigation into the impact of care modalities on neonatal health and the long-term implications for infant development.
